notes: high
Basil masks Capsicum scent from pests and repels some aphids and flies. Recommend interplanting.
Carrots grow deep (taproot) while tomatoes grow shallow — complementary root zones. Neither crowds the other; carrots break up soil benefiting tomato roots.
Root-depth complementarity; carrots go deep
Tomatoes and brassicas have conflicting root exudates and compete for N; tomatoes also shade brassicas
Armoracia rusticana (horseradish) planted at row edges reputed to repel Colorado potato beetle on Solanum tuberosum; primarily a border/repellent plant strategy.
Beans fix atmospheric N benefiting nitrogen-demanding eggplant; beans grow vertically and do not shade eggplant significantly; eggplant shades beans slightly — slight one-way benefit.

Corn canopy shades tomato sufficiently to reduce fruit set in many conditions; the negative effect is primarily height/canopy shading in the same bed; if grown as 3-sisters with beans it can benefit but direct tomato-corn proximity is generally negative.
Both Solanaceae subject to same pests (Colorado potato beetle
Some sources recommend celery shading eggplant during summer heat; evidence is limited and specific to hot-climate conditions; included at low confidence only.

Fennel and related Apiaceae produce allelopathic compounds that stunt growth of many vegetables including Capsicum; if applied sparingly as a border herb the effect may be mild but direct proximity is generally inhibitory.
Onions/alliums slow growth and reduce yield of common bean (Phaseolus vulgaris). Documented in multiple companion-planting lists.
Clover as living mulch smothers weeds around brassicas
Basil and thyme have overlapping pest-repellent effects against some flies and beetles; evidence for the pairing specifically is mostly anecdotal; both benefit from similar cultural conditions.
Both aromatic Lamiaceae — shared pest-repellent effect against whiteflies/aphids; mostly same-culture herb bed combinations.
Rosemary reputed to repel bean beetle (bean weevil) when grown near beans; also repels cabbage moth and carrot fly. Specifically noted for Phaseolus.
Rosemary masks cabbage scent from cabbage moth and repels flea beetles on brassicas. Mulch/repellent effect.
Sage repels carrot fly (Psila rosae) — noted in European carrot-growing guides. Low-care pairing.
Thyme masks cabbage scent from cabbage worms and imported cabbage butterfly (Pieris rapae). Established repellant pairing.
Lavender repels many brassica pests including flea beetle and cabbage moth. Aromatic companion effect.

Dill (Foeniculum vulgare common garden dill) attracts beneficial parasitoid wasps and hoverflies that prey on carrot fly and aphids. carrot-flies are a classic nemesis of Daucus carota; dill as trap/distraction crop is established.
Celery and carrot competing for same soil zone and similar N needs; celery shade may stunt carrot tops — generally recommended to separate.
Dill attracts beneficial insects and some aphid predators that benefit brassicas; dill grown near brassicas historically recommended.
Parsley claimed to attract beneficial insects but some sources warn it competes with beans for soil nutrients; evidence weak; listed at low confidence as an uncertain/possibly negative pairing.
Coriander attracts predatory insects beneficial near lettuce; supported by many herb-vegetable companion guides.
Glechoma (ground ivy) aggressively spreads and can smother lettuce in garden beds; allelopathic potential unclear but widely considered weedy groundcover; caution noted.
